title: Lesson 420
transcript: Okay, I just talked about hardening at a high level. I want to go into just a bit more detail about what hardening is all about and why it's so useful and how we use it and et cetera, so forth. So as I mentioned, fundamentally, hardening is all about locking down a system. Because when you go and buy some new application, some new server, operating system, whatever, firewall, let's pick on a firewall. When you go and buy a new firewall, does it come from the manufacturer all locked down, hardened, and super secure? No. Why not? Well, because when you get some brand new application or hardware, you need to be able to log into it for the first time. So there has to be a default username and password. And on the firewall, a bunch of the ports have to be open so you can remotely log into it. And it has to allow any IP address to log into it. When you buy something from a manufacturer, in almost all cases, it's going to come from the manufacturer in a very insecure state. And this is, of course, where hardening comes in. Before you put anything into production, you need to go through a process to lock that thing down, harden it before you put it into production. So that's what I want to talk about in just a little bit more detail here. Hardening is a very simple process. Hardening is just where you essentially go through a checklist of things that you need to do to lock something down to harden it. This list is obviously going to vary depending on what it is, whether it's a Cisco firewall, or a Palo Alto firewall, or the Windows operating system, or the Unix operating system, or whatever. The list is going to vary, but it's going to be stuff like, you know, change the default username and password, install the most recent patches, close these ports, disable that, etc. So fundamentally, hardening is just following a checklist and doing all the things on the checklist to lock something down. where do you get this checklist well one possible source is from the manufacturer themselves so windows microsoft for instance develops hardening and provides hardening guides for their windows operating systems cisco provides hardening guides for a bunch of their different network devices so sometimes you can get a hardening guide from the manufacturer themselves There's also a big open source repository. The CIS, the Center for Internet Security, maintains a big open source database of hardening guides. So you might be able to get one of these things from a manufacturer. You might be able to get it from an open source repository like CIS. Or you might just have to create one from scratch. No matter where you get a hardening guide from, especially if you get it from CIS or the manufacturer, you're going to have to tailor the hardening guide to your exact environment and exactly how you're using that server, application, whatever. Because obviously the way that you harden a system that's being used, like if you're using a web server in this environment with this data in it versus a web server here with this data in it, they're going to need different rules. They're going to need different controls in place to lock them down for different usages of them. So you're going to have to create a hardening guide for each type of system, each business use case for a system. And then, like I said, hardening is actually a very simple process. When you acquire something new, you just, as part of hardening it, you just go through that checklist. Disable the unnecessary services, enable a host-based firewall, whatever. You typically want two people involved in this process. You want the system administrator, the system administrator or the person that's setting the sync thing up, they'll go through and they'll harden it. And maybe they've even created some scripts to automate this. You know, people, system administrators, we're fallible, so are scripts. There might be a mistake in them. So you typically want a second person involved. So after the system administrator has hardened the system, you then usually want a second person, often from the compliance function, that will double check to make sure, okay, were all the steps followed? Has this system been properly hardened? before it goes into production. So that's why I usually want two people involved. The person actually doing the hardening, and then often someone from the compliance function just doing a final check to say, did we get everything? Or did we miss something? You can then put now this hardened, locked down device, you can now put it into production. But you're not done with hardening once something's in production. You need to do ongoing monitoring to make sure the system remains hardened for a couple of major reasons. One, you might update your hardening guide. Over time, you might realize, oh, we need to install a new patch, or we need to change this setting, or we need to add a new service account, or whatever. or disable some service account. So over time, your hardening guide will change, will evolve. And so obviously, any new systems you're going to deploy, you want to make sure that they're deployed according to your updated hardening guide. But you've also got to monitor and make sure that any of your systems that are already in production are appropriately updated to maintain compliance with the hardening guide. So that's one reason to do monitoring, to make sure that if you update your hardening guide, you update any of your systems in production. Another reason is that you want to monitor to make sure a system remains in compliance with the hardening guide is that a change might be made to a system in production that takes it out of compliance with the hardening guide. And you want to detect that. Was it one of your own system administrators that did this? Or was it maybe an attacker installing some sort of backdoor that opened up a port on the system? So by doing this ongoing monitoring to make sure a system remains in compliance, it might help you detect if something untoward has happened with your system. So that's why you need to do ongoing monitoring. to make sure that systems that are in production are appropriately hardened. And by the way, all of this fits within overall configuration management. Because of course, what is configuration management? Making sure the systems are properly configured before they go into production and then remain appropriately configured while they're in production. So hardening very much fits within overall configuration management.
